Answer: An open-source platform that uses containerization technology to package applications and their dependencies into lightweight portable units called containers.
A running instance of an application isolated from the rest of your system. Think of it as a lightweight virtual machine but faster and sharing the host OS kernel.
A read-only blueprint/snapshot used to create containers. It packages your app + its dependencies + OS libraries into one bundle. You run an image to get a container.
A text file with step by step instructions to build an image. Like a recipe: start from Ubuntu install Python copy my code run this command.

A way to persist data outside the container lifecycle. Since containers are ephemeral (data dies when they do) volumes mount a folder from your host machine into the container so data survives restarts.

A tool to define and run multiple containers together using a single docker-compose.yml file. Instead of starting each container manually you describe all services and spin them all up with one command: docker compose up.
A web server and reverse proxy. It receives HTTP requests from browsers and serves files or forwards requests to your app. Known for being fast and lightweight. Alternative to Apache.
An open-source relational database and a fork of MySQL. Stores data in tables with rows and columns. Used by WordPress to store posts users settings etc.
A PHP-based CMS (Content Management System). It is a web app that lets you build and manage websites without coding. It talks to a database (MariaDB/MySQL) to store content and serves pages via PHP.
HyperText Transfer Protocol. The rules that browsers and servers use to communicate. Data is sent in plain text so anyone intercepting the traffic can read it. Runs on port 80.
HTTP + encryption. Same protocol but wrapped in TLS so data is encrypted in transit. Nobody can intercept and read it. Runs on port 443.
Secure Sockets Layer. The old encryption protocol for securing web traffic. It is now deprecated and replaced by TLS but people still say SSL casually when they mean TLS.
Transport Layer Security. The modern secure replacement for SSL. When you visit an HTTPS site TLS is what is actually encrypting the connection. You get a TLS certificate to prove your site identity.
A server-side scripting language. WordPress is written in PHP. When someone visits your site the server runs PHP code which dynamically builds the HTML page and returns it to the browser.

PHP FastCGI Process Manager. A smarter way to run PHP. Instead of running PHP inside the web server PHP-FPM runs PHP as a separate service (pool of worker processes). NGINX hands off PHP requests to PHP-FPM to execute.

A protocol for communication between a web server (NGINX) and an external process (like PHP-FPM). NGINX does not run PHP itself — it speaks FastCGI to pass the request to PHP-FPM and get the response back.
The actual database engine process that runs on your machine/container. It listens for connections stores data and executes queries. This is what you install when you want to host a database.
A command-line tool (mysql or mariadb) you use to connect to a MariaDB server and run SQL queries manually. It is just an interface — it does not store anything itself.
The background process (mysqld) that is the running MySQL/MariaDB server. "d" stands for daemon. It sits silently in the background waiting for database connections.

A background process that runs silently without user interaction. It starts (usually at boot) and just waits to do its job serving requests handling connections etc. Examples: nginx (web daemon) mysqld (database daemon) sshd (SSH daemon).

A command-line toolkit and library for all things cryptography. You use it to generate TLS certificates create private keys test HTTPS connections and encrypt/decrypt files. It powers TLS under the hood on most Linux systems.

WordPress Command Line Interface. A tool to manage WordPress from the terminal — install plugins update WordPress create users reset passwords run database operations — all without touching the browser. Essential for automation and Docker setups.
